metadata_frame: The following passage comes from Round the World in Silence, a memoir published in 1911 by Annabelle Kent, an American .
main_question:
There were more idols there than we had seen anywhere. Nearly every house had its little outside shrine, with a hideous red idol and bunch of flowers. From hot and dusty streets, our carriages turned into the green enclosure of the Memorial Gardens. They are not large, [masked clause revising an implied expectation]. Each carriage is required to proceed at a walking pace, in accordance with the solemnity of the place.

Write a clause appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked clause revising an implied expectation]:
answer_strings: but are well kept and very beautiful
but are neglected and quite ordinary
but we had a good deal of difficulty in making our carriage drivers understand what we wanted
but we were not allowed to go further than the garden
but they are beautifully maintained and serene
they are instead modest, a deliberate counterpoint to the surrounding bustle
passage: There were more idols there than we had seen anywhere. Nearly every house had its little outside shrine, with a hideous red idol and bunch of flowers. From hot and dusty streets, our carriages turned into the green enclosure of the Memorial Gardens. They are not large, but are well kept and very beautiful. Each carriage is required to proceed at a walking pace, in accordance with the solemnity of the place.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from Round the World in Silence, a memoir published in 1911 by Annabelle Kent, an American .
main_question:
Sunday:-Here we are in the Bay of Naples. Yesterday they woke me before daylight to look out at Vesuvius. The silvery grey-blue water was banked by dark blue mountains, and behind the mountains. the sky glowed red. Did you know they have strikes in Italy? They do. As we were about to go ashore this morning, we heard that it was uncertain when the Irene would proceed to Genoa, [masked clause describing a cause or reason]. After waiting around for an hour we decided to land, for a while at least.

Write a clause appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked clause describing a cause or reason]:
answer_strings: because there was a strike among the deck hands, and the cargo could not be unloaded
because the deck hands were on good terms with management, allowing the cargo to be unloaded efficiently
for the coolies invariably say "yes", whether they understand or not
due to the dockworkers' strike
because the port workers were on strike, disrupting the ship's schedule
passage: Sunday:-Here we are in the Bay of Naples. Yesterday they woke me before daylight to look out at Vesuvius. The silvery grey-blue water was banked by dark blue mountains, and behind the mountains. the sky glowed red. Did you know they have strikes in Italy? They do. As we were about to go ashore this morning, we heard that it was uncertain when the Irene would proceed to Genoa, because there was a strike among the deck hands, and the cargo could not be unloaded. After waiting around for an hour we decided to land, for a while at least.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from Round the World in Silence, a memoir published in 1911 by Annabelle Kent, an American .
main_question:
So in I got, and my wily coachman drove away and up to the stable, where, instead of changing the horse, he turned around and started for Nan Yang. As I knew it would be useless to remonstrate, I held my peace and waited to see what happened. [masked sentence revising an implied expectation] You never saw anything as crooked as these Chinese streets.

Write a sentence appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked sentence revising an implied expectation]:
answer_strings: The brute, however, developed a surprising speed and endurance, so I settled down to enjoy the ride.
The brute, however, slowed peaceably, so I settled down to endure the ride.
However, I was alive and ready to see all there was to be seen when we came into the harbor.
I had expected to be taken back to the inn but it was not to be.
I had expected the coachman to simply change the horse, but instead he had a different destination in mind.
passage: So in I got, and my wily coachman drove away and up to the stable, where, instead of changing the horse, he turned around and started for Nan Yang. As I knew it would be useless to remonstrate, I held my peace and waited to see what happened. The brute, however, developed a surprising speed and endurance, so I settled down to enjoy the ride. You never saw anything as crooked as these Chinese streets.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from Round the World in Silence, a memoir published in 1911 by Annabelle Kent, an American .
main_question:
There was a large party of us, and we engaged a guide, one who was very proud of his English. After we had looked into the Aquarium and a few shops, we turned toward Pompeii to spend the rest of the day there. We were all crowded into one wagonette, and the sun was hot, [masked clause describing an inference or effect].

Write a clause appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked clause describing an inference or effect]:
answer_strings: so we were glad enough to stop for luncheon at an out-of-door restaurant
so we were glad enough to press on without pausing at an out-of-door restaurant
so I saw very little of Shanghai before coming down here
so we had to eat our lunch on the boat
making us eager to find shade amidst the ancient ruins
so we felt the oppressive heat pressing down on us
passage: There was a large party of us, and we engaged a guide, one who was very proud of his English. After we had looked into the Aquarium and a few shops, we turned toward Pompeii to spend the rest of the day there. We were all crowded into one wagonette, and the sun was hot, so we were glad enough to stop for luncheon at an out-of-door restaurant.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from Round the World in Silence, a memoir published in 1911 by Annabelle Kent, an American .
main_question:
We wanted to see the Cathedral and the Museum ; but the man was so stupid we could do nothing with him, and after driving around in purposeless fashion for an hour or so, in the vain hope that a ray of light would dawn upon him, we gave it up and went home, deciding on the way to leave for Bangalore the next evening. [masked sentence describing an inference or effect]

Write a sentence appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked sentence describing an inference or effect]:
answer_strings: So my memories of Madras will be roads, dripping trees, and long stretches of stuccoed garden walls of a uniform discolored and mouldy yellow, all seen from the front seat of a gharry.
Our frustration with the local guide left us feeling exhausted and disheartened.
His obstinacy left us feeling helpless, and the hour slipped away like a mirage, the day dissolving into an aimless haze that made our plans feel futile for the rest of the afternoon.
passage: We wanted to see the Cathedral and the Museum ; but the man was so stupid we could do nothing with him, and after driving around in purposeless fashion for an hour or so, in the vain hope that a ray of light would dawn upon him, we gave it up and went home, deciding on the way to leave for Bangalore the next evening. So my memories of Madras will be roads, dripping trees, and long stretches of stuccoed garden walls of a uniform discolored and mouldy yellow, all seen from the front seat of a gharry.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from Round the World in Silence, a memoir published in 1911 by Annabelle Kent, an American .
main_question:
At last the three days wore away, and early on Sunday morning we anchored off Penang. An hour later, when the tender came, we were all ready to leave, with no lingering this time. The harbor was full of queer little Chinese rowboats, with huge eyes painted on the stern. You know the Chinese say their boats can't see where they are going, [masked clause describing a condition or proviso].

Write a clause appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked clause describing a condition or proviso]:
answer_strings: if they have no eyes
even if they possess sight
if it were not for the Arab and French servants
unless someone guides them with an oar
unless they are steered by a seasoned captain
passage: At last the three days wore away, and early on Sunday morning we anchored off Penang. An hour later, when the tender came, we were all ready to leave, with no lingering this time. The harbor was full of queer little Chinese rowboats, with huge eyes painted on the stern. You know the Chinese say their boats can't see where they are going, if they have no eyes.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from Round the World in Silence, a memoir published in 1911 by Annabelle Kent, an American .
main_question:
The steamer waited at Port Said until afternoon for the arrival of the Osiris with the mails, and all morning the decks looked like an Oriental bazaar, for the people from shore were allowed to bring their wares on board and there was such an array of jewelry, laces, embroidery, glittering silver-wrought shawls, and postal cards spread out on the deck, that we had to move carefully not to step on something. Over the side, boys were diving for pennies, and boats with serenaders surrounded us. About two o'clock the decks were cleared, and we began to move. It took us all night to go through the Canal, [masked clause acknowledging a countervailing fact]. The man who knows everything told us it costs $10,000 to get a ship the size of the China through, and that it was not De Lesseps, but an Englishman named Waghorn who conceived the idea of that canal, but as he died some years before the work was begun, De Lesseps had all the glory.

Write a clause appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked clause acknowledging a countervailing fact]:
answer_strings: though it is only a hundred miles long
though it stretches for hundreds of miles
though our quarters were most airy and comfortable
though the heat of the sun was so intense that I could hardly endure it
despite the fact that it was a slow and tedious process
though the canal offered a swift passage
passage: The steamer waited at Port Said until afternoon for the arrival of the Osiris with the mails, and all morning the decks looked like an Oriental bazaar, for the people from shore were allowed to bring their wares on board and there was such an array of jewelry, laces, embroidery, glittering silver-wrought shawls, and postal cards spread out on the deck, that we had to move carefully not to step on something. Over the side, boys were diving for pennies, and boats with serenaders surrounded us. About two o'clock the decks were cleared, and we began to move. It took us all night to go through the Canal, though it is only a hundred miles long. The man who knows everything told us it costs $10,000 to get a ship the size of the China through, and that it was not De Lesseps, but an Englishman named Waghorn who conceived the idea of that canal, but as he died some years before the work was begun, De Lesseps had all the glory.
